summary:
The founding of the Khmer Angkor civilization (c. 802 AD) is traditionally dated to the declaration by Jayavarman II of his independence from Java and his consecration as a 'universal monarch' (chakravartin) and 'god-king' (devaraja) on the sacred mountain of Phnom Kulen, establishing the religious and political ideology that would animate the construction of Angkor over the following five centuries. Jayavarman II unified the competing [[event:2962|pre-Angkorian polities]] of the Cambodian plain and established his capital near the northern shore of the Tonle Sap lake — the most productive freshwater fishery in Southeast Asia — in the region that would become Angkor.

The genius of Angkor was hydraulic as much as architectural. The Cambodian plain's flat terrain and seasonal monsoon created a challenging agricultural environment: the Tonle Sap floods dramatically in the wet season and shrinks in the dry season, and successful intensive rice cultivation required controlling this water through an engineered landscape of reservoirs (barays), canals, and water distribution networks that could store monsoon water for dry-season irrigation. The Angkor hydraulic system, developed progressively from the 9th through 12th centuries, supported one of the largest pre-industrial urban populations in Southeast Asia — modern LiDAR surveys have revealed that Greater Angkor at its height (12th–13th century) may have covered an area larger than modern Los Angeles, with a population potentially exceeding one million. The temples of Angkor — culminating in [[event:2168|Angkor Wat]] (12th century) — expressed the cosmic and political ideology of the god-king in stone, each temple-mountain representing Mount Meru and the king's personal relationship with the divine.
